text:
This paper presents the results of archaeological rescue excavations conducted between 2009 and 2014 in the northern part of Prague's New Town, focusing on the "Jewish Garden," the largest medieval Jewish necropolis in Bohemia. Active from the mid-13th to the late 15th century, the site underwent significant transformation following its closure in 1478. Recent field research, primarily prompted by the construction of the Quadrio multifunctional complex, has provided critical data to revise the northern and northwestern boundaries of the cemetery, which extended further north than previously assumed.

The findings reveal a complex settlement mosaic, indicating that the cemetery was established over an earlier 12th-century settlement horizon associated with the village of Újezd of St. Martin. Variations in burial density were observed, with the highest concentration of graves located in the central area, while the periphery was defined by a dry moat and specific boundary structures. In addition to skeletal remains, the excavations recovered a significant collection of 24 sandstone tombstone fragments dating from the 14th to the 15th century. Notably, the discovery of unfinished stelae provides evidence of a local stonemasonry workshop specializing in Jewish funerary monuments. These results enhance our understanding of the spatial evolution and ritual practices of the medieval Jewish community in Prague and underscore the necessity of ongoing archaeological monitoring in this culturally sensitive urban area.
